summary: Aristotle outlines strategies for the Arguments section of a speech, distinguishing between forensic, political, and ceremonial oratory. He provides tactical advice on the use of logical proofs (enthymemes), the importance of moral character, and how to sequence arguments to effectively dismantle an opponent's case.

principles.statement: Different types of oratory require different forms of proof based on their relationship to time.
principles.rationale: Political oratory deals with the contingent future, making past examples the best proof. Forensic oratory deals with the non-contingent past or present, making logical demonstration (enthymemes) more suitable.
principles.source_quote: Argument by "example" is highly suitable for political oratory, argument by "enthymeme" better suits forensic. Political oratory deals with future events... Forensic oratory deals with what is or is not now true, which can better be demonstrated, because not contingent

principles.statement: Refutative arguments are more persuasive than demonstrative ones.
principles.rationale: When two opposing facts are placed side by side, their logical cogency becomes much more striking and clear to the audience.
principles.source_quote: Refutative enthymemes are more popular than demonstrative ones: their logical cogency is more striking: the facts about two opposites always stand out clearly when the two are nut side by side.

principles.statement: Displaying good moral character is more persuasive than displaying subtle logic.
principles.rationale: Audiences are more receptive to a speaker who appears to be an honest, good person than one who relies solely on complex reasoning.
principles.source_quote: after all, it is more fitting for a good man to display himself as an honest fellow than as a subtle reasoner.

principles.statement: You must clear the audience's mind of your opponent's arguments before presenting your own.
principles.rationale: Just as people are prejudiced against a person they dislike, an audience's mind will refuse a favorable reception to your speech if they have already been favorably impressed by the opposition.
principles.source_quote: You should, therefore, make room in the minds of the audience for your coming speech; and this will be done by getting your opponent's speech out of the way.

frameworks.name: The Four Heads of Dispute
frameworks.when_to_apply: When determining the main task of your argument in a court case.
steps: Determine if you are arguing that the act was not committed.
Determine if you are arguing that the act did no harm.
Determine if you are arguing that the act was less severe than alleged.
Determine if you are arguing that the act was justified.
frameworks.source_quote: (1) If you maintain that the act was not committed , your main task in court is to prove this. (2) If you maintain that the act did no harm , prove this. If you maintain that (3) the act was less than is alleged, or (4) justified , prove these facts

frameworks.name: Sequencing the Reply to the Opponent
frameworks.when_to_apply: When structuring a speech in a political assembly or court, depending on your speaking order.
steps: If you are the first speaker: Put your own arguments forward first.
Then, meet the anticipated arguments of the other side by refuting them beforehand.
If you speak later: Begin by refuting your opponent's speech (especially the most successful points) to clear the audience's mind.
frameworks.source_quote: if you are the first speaker you should put your own arguments forward first, and then meet the arguments on the other side by refuting them... If you speak later, you must first, by means of refutation and counter-syllogism, attempt some answer to your opponent's speech

mental_models.name: Simultaneous Motions Cancel Out
mental_models.description: A psychological model suggesting that cognitive processes (logical demonstration) and emotional processes (rousing feeling) are opposing 'motions' in the mind. Applying both at the same time causes them to neutralize each other.
mental_models.example: Avoiding the use of enthymemes (logical proofs) when trying to rouse an audience's emotions, because the logic will kill the feeling.

mental_models.name: The Prejudiced Mind as a Container
mental_models.description: The audience's mind has limited capacity and can be 'filled' by an opponent's speech. To persuade them, a speaker must first 'make room' by dismantling the opponent's arguments before pouring in their own.
mental_models.example: Attacking the opponent's most vulnerable or successful points first before introducing your own case.

heuristics: Note any falsehoods about irrelevant matters to make the opponent's relevant statements look false too.
If you find yourself in difficulties during a political speech, attack your adversary to divert the audience's attention.
Put risky assertions about yourself, or abusive remarks about your opponent, into the mouth of a third person.
Restate your logical enthymemes in the form of pithy maxims to express moral character.

signature_quotes.text: Argument by "example" is highly suitable for political oratory, argument by "enthymeme" better suits forensic.
signature_quotes.context: Distinguishing the types of proof needed based on whether the speech deals with the future or the past.

signature_quotes.text: after all, it is more fitting for a good man to display himself as an honest fellow than as a subtle reasoner.
signature_quotes.context: Advising speakers to fall back on moral discourse if they lack logical proofs.

signature_quotes.text: You should, therefore, make room in the minds of the audience for your coming speech; and this will be done by getting your opponent's speech out of the way.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why speakers who go second must refute their opponent before making their own case.

signature_quotes.text: Refutative enthymemes are more popular than demonstrative ones: their logical cogency is more striking
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the power of contrasting two opposites side by side.

anti_patterns.description: Using a continuous succession of logical proofs (enthymemes).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: They will spoil one another's effect. They must be interspersed with other matter to remain effective.

anti_patterns.description: Trying to make logical proofs (enthymemes) on every single point.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It makes you sound like a student of philosophy whose conclusions are more familiar and believable than the premises used to draw them.

anti_patterns.description: Using logical proofs (enthymemes) when trying to rouse feeling or depict character.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It will kill the feeling or fall flat because simultaneous motions cancel each other out, and the process of demonstration cannot express moral character.

anti_patterns.description: Making boastful assertions about yourself or directly abusing your opponent.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It excites dislike, appears tedious, exposes you to contradiction, and makes you seem ill-bred.
